fields.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
fields.definition: A psychiatric disorder that can develop in people who have experienced or witnessed a traumatic event, or who have learned that a traumatic event occurred to a close person. PTSD is characterized by intrusive symptoms (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, negative changes in thoughts and mood, and heightened arousal and reactivity. Symptoms must persist for more than 1 month and cause significant distress or functional impairment. PTSD was previously classified as an anxiety disorder; since DSM-5, it has its own diagnostic category: Trauma- and Stressor-Related Disorders.
fields.example: A combat veteran returns home but cannot sleep, re-experiences battle sounds as flashbacks when hearing loud noises, avoids crowded places, and feels emotionally numb to family.

fields.name: Traumatic Event Types (PTSD Criterion A)
fields.definition: For a PTSD diagnosis, the person must have been exposed to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence in one of four ways: (1) Direct experience — the person was directly involved in the traumatic event; (2) Witnessing — in person, seeing it happen to others; (3) Learning about it — discovering that a violent or accidental traumatic event happened to a close family member or friend; (4) Repeated or extreme indirect exposure — such as first responders repeatedly exposed to graphic details of trauma (does not apply to media exposure). The exposure must involve actual or threatened death, injury, or sexual violence.
fields.example: A first responder who repeatedly processes crime scenes, a parent who learns their child was sexually assaulted, a bystander who witnessed a violent assault — all qualify under Criterion A.

fields.name: Intrusion Symptoms (PTSD)
fields.definition: One of four DSM-5 PTSD symptom clusters. Intrusion symptoms involve unwanted re-experiencing of the traumatic event. They include: flashbacks — reliving the trauma as if it is happening again, including physical sensations like racing heart and sweating; recurrent, distressing memories of the event; recurring distressing dreams related to the trauma; intense psychological distress when exposed to cues that symbolize the trauma; and marked physiological reactions to internal or external trauma-related cues. At least one intrusion symptom is required for a PTSD diagnosis.
fields.example: A car accident survivor experiences a flashback — complete with fear, rapid heartbeat, and the physical sensation of impact — whenever they hear brakes screech.

fields.name: Avoidance Symptoms (PTSD)
fields.definition: One of four DSM-5 PTSD symptom clusters. Avoidance involves persistent efforts to avoid distressing trauma-related stimuli. It includes: internal avoidance — avoiding thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations associated with the traumatic event; and external avoidance — avoiding external reminders such as people, places, conversations, activities, objects, or situations that trigger trauma-related distress. At least one avoidance symptom is required for PTSD diagnosis. Avoidance provides short-term relief but prevents processing of the trauma and maintains PTSD long-term.
fields.example: A sexual assault survivor avoids the neighborhood where the assault occurred, cannot watch any TV content involving violence, and shuts down conversations about safety or relationships.

fields.name: Negative Alterations in Cognition and Mood (PTSD)
fields.definition: One of four DSM-5 PTSD symptom clusters (Criterion D), introduced in DSM-5 to separate what had been grouped with avoidance in DSM-IV. Includes: inability to remember an important aspect of the traumatic event; persistent and exaggerated negative beliefs about oneself, others, or the world ('I am broken,' 'No one can be trusted'); distorted self-blame or blame of others for the trauma; persistent negative emotional states (fear, horror, anger, guilt, shame); markedly diminished interest in activities; feeling detached or estranged from others; and persistent inability to experience positive emotions. At least two symptoms from this cluster are required.
fields.example: After a trauma, a person is convinced they caused it, cannot feel joy or connection with loved ones, and believes the world is entirely dangerous.

fields.name: Arousal and Reactivity Symptoms (PTSD)
fields.definition: One of four DSM-5 PTSD symptom clusters (Criterion E). Reflects persistent changes in arousal and reactivity that developed after the trauma. Symptoms include: irritable behavior and angry outbursts (often with little provocation); reckless or self-destructive behavior; hypervigilance — being in a constant state of alertness to threat; exaggerated startle response; problems concentrating; and sleep disturbance (difficulty falling or staying asleep, or restless sleep). At least two arousal/reactivity symptoms are required for PTSD diagnosis.
fields.example: A trauma survivor cannot sleep in a room with the door closed, constantly scans exits in public spaces, and responds to a dropped book with a full alarm reaction.

fields.name: Prolonged Exposure Therapy (PE)
fields.definition: A highly effective, evidence-based treatment for PTSD developed by Dr. Edna Foa. PE involves two primary techniques: imaginal exposure — repeatedly revisiting and recounting the traumatic memory in detail in a therapeutic context, allowing the emotional response to the memory to reduce through habituation; and in vivo exposure — gradually approaching real-world situations or places that have been avoided because they trigger trauma-related distress. PE is typically delivered in 8–15 sessions and has strong research support from NIMH and VA/DoD. Brain imaging research has identified the neural circuits on which PE acts to reduce PTSD symptoms.
fields.example: A rape survivor works with a therapist to recount the assault in detail repeatedly across sessions, and gradually returns to the neighborhood where it occurred — reducing avoidance and intrusion symptoms over 12 weeks.

fields.name: Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR)
fields.definition: An evidence-based trauma therapy in which the person recalls distressing traumatic memories while simultaneously following a therapist's finger moving back and forth (or other bilateral stimulation such as tapping or tones). EMDR helps desensitize traumatic memories by reducing their emotional charge. The bilateral stimulation component is thought to facilitate the processing and integration of traumatic memories into long-term memory. EMDR is recognized by NIMH, VA/DoD, and the World Health Organization as an effective PTSD treatment. It is particularly useful for individuals who have difficulty verbally processing trauma.
fields.example: A person with PTSD holds a distressing trauma image in mind while following a therapist's moving finger; after multiple sets, the image's emotional intensity is noticeably reduced.

fields.body: EMDR proceeds through 8 phases. After history-taking and preparation, the therapist identifies specific traumatic memories and associated negative beliefs to target. The patient holds the distressing image and accompanying negative cognition in mind while tracking the therapist's moving finger (or receiving auditory or tactile bilateral stimulation). After each set of bilateral stimulation, the patient reports what arose. The therapist guides the patient to process whatever surfaces — memories, sensations, emotions — without forcing a particular outcome. Over repeated sets, the emotional charge of the target memory decreases (desensitization) and the associated negative belief is replaced with a more adaptive one (reprocessing/installation). Body scan and closure phases address any remaining physical tension. The process continues across sessions until the target memory no longer provokes significant distress.
fields.name: How EMDR Works
fields.example: A patient holds the memory of a car accident and follows finger movements; after several sets, the memory feels more distant and the belief 'I should have died' is replaced with 'I survived and I am safe now.'

fields.name: Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT)
fields.definition: An evidence-based cognitive therapy for PTSD that focuses on identifying and challenging 'stuck points' — unhelpful beliefs formed as a result of the trauma, such as self-blame, distorted views of safety, trust, power/control, esteem, and intimacy. CPT is typically delivered in 12 sessions. Patients write a trauma account (in standard CPT) and use structured worksheets to examine the evidence for and against their stuck-point beliefs. CPT is one of the most well-researched PTSD treatments and is effective for PTSD arising from any trauma type. It can be delivered individually or in group format.
fields.example: A combat veteran who believes 'I am responsible for my unit's deaths' works through CPT worksheets to examine the facts of the situation and develop a more accurate understanding of what occurred.

fields.question: How many US adults have PTSD in a given year?
fields.answer: Approximately 3.6% of US adults had PTSD in the past year based on national survey data cited by NIMH. About 6 in every 100 people will experience PTSD at some point in their lifetime. About half of all US adults experience at least one traumatic event, but most do not develop PTSD — suggesting that individual factors such as biology, prior trauma history, and social support influence risk.

fields.question: How long must PTSD symptoms persist for a diagnosis?
fields.answer: All required symptoms must be present for more than 1 month. The full DSM-5 criteria require at least 1 intrusion symptom, 1 avoidance symptom, 2 negative cognition/mood symptoms, and 2 arousal/reactivity symptoms — all for more than 1 month — along with significant distress or functional impairment. If all criteria are met but the duration is less than 1 month, a diagnosis of Acute Stress Disorder may apply instead.

fields.question: What is the difference between Acute Stress Disorder and PTSD?
fields.answer: Both involve trauma-related symptoms, but the key difference is timing and duration. Acute Stress Disorder (ASD) is diagnosed when trauma-related symptoms occur within 3 days to 4 weeks following a traumatic event and last less than 1 month. If symptoms persist beyond 1 month and meet full PTSD criteria, the diagnosis changes to PTSD. Not all people with ASD develop PTSD, but having ASD is a risk factor for subsequent PTSD. Early intervention following ASD may reduce PTSD development.

fields.title: PTSD and Co-Occurring Conditions
fields.body: PTSD rarely presents in isolation. People with PTSD frequently have one or more co-occurring conditions that complicate both diagnosis and treatment. Common co-occurring conditions include: Major Depressive Disorder — depression is present in a majority of people with PTSD and shares overlapping symptoms (emotional numbing, sleep disturbance, loss of interest); Substance Use Disorders — research estimates 30–60% of people with PTSD also have alcohol or drug use disorders, often as attempts to self-medicate intrusion and arousal symptoms; Other anxiety disorders; Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) — especially in military populations, where blast injuries and PTSD frequently co-occur; and Chronic pain — particularly in survivors of physical injury. The good news: evidence-based PTSD treatments (PE, CPT, EMDR) can be safely used even when these co-occurring conditions are present, and treating PTSD often reduces co-occurring symptoms as well.

fields.question: Does PTSD only affect combat veterans or military personnel?
fields.answer: No. NIMH explicitly states: "Anyone can develop PTSD at any age. This includes combat veterans and people who have experienced or witnessed a physical or sexual assault, abuse, an accident, a disaster, a terror attack, or other serious events." NIMH also notes that learning about a close friend or relative's traumatic experience can cause PTSD. Approximately 6 out of every 100 people will experience PTSD during their lifetime — the vast majority are not veterans. Framing PTSD as exclusively a military condition delays recognition and treatment for the many civilians who need help.
